<!-- Topic 2: What's the Output? -->
<!-- Total slides: 4 -->

# What's the Output?

---

## Exercise 1 — What does this print? {.smaller}

+ Two variables, one reference, one modification. What does each `cout` print?

::: notes
Total slides: 4
Give students 60 seconds to work this out on paper before revealing.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Exercise 1 — Code {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int x = 10;
    int& r = x;

    r = 20;
    cout << x << "\n";    // line A

    int y = 30;
    int* p = &x;
    p = &y;
    *p = 50;

    cout << x << "\n";    // line B
    cout << y << "\n";    // line C

    return 0;
}
```

::: notes
Line A: 20 — r is an alias for x; assigning to r changes x.
Line B: 20 — p was reseated to y; x is untouched.
Line C: 50 — *p = 50 modifies y through the pointer.
:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Exercise 2 — What does this print? {.smaller}

+ A reference parameter and a pointer parameter side by side. Trace each call.

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Exercise 2 — Code {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

void doubleRef(int& n) { n *= 2; }
void doublePtr(int* n) { *n *= 2; }

int main() {
    int a = 5;
    int b = 5;

    doubleRef(a);
    doublePtr(&b);

    cout << a << "\n";    // line A
    cout << b << "\n";    // line B

    int c = 3;
    int* p = &c;
    doublePtr(p);
    cout << c << "\n";    // line C

    return 0;
}
```

::: notes
Line A: 10 — doubleRef takes a reference; a is modified in place.
Line B: 10 — doublePtr takes a pointer; *n *= 2 modifies b through the pointer.
Line C: 6 — p holds the address of c; doublePtr(p) passes that address, *n *= 2 modifies c.
:::

<!-- Slide 4 -->
